title: The Daimon Contact Path through Layayoga and Tantraloka

sources.label: Shyam Sundar Goswami — Layayoga (1980/1999): The Definitive Guide to the Chakras and Kundalini
PGM VII.505-528 — Meeting with Your Own Daimon
Liber Samekh (Crowley) — Theurgia Goetia Summa Congressus Cum Daemone
Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 1 (Āhnika 1), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski
Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 3 (Āhnika 4), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski
Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 4 (Āhnika 5-6), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski

body.text: The daimon-name LVJK, when received through Liber Samekh's 'voces magicae' tradition, operates as a bīja-mantra in the Layayoga sense. Goswami teaches that a mantra, when made living (mantra-caitanya), manifests as the very form of the Iṣṭadevatā — the chosen deity. The process by which the sound-structure of the mantra becomes the living being mirrors the daimon contact journey: the name is not a label but a seed that germinates into the full presence of the daimon. The fifty Mātṛkā phonemes from A to Kṣa, being Śiva-Śakti in sound-form, provide the sonic substrate through which the barbarous names operate on the subtle body.

The Abramelin purification (six-month retreat, graduated cleansing) and Acher's Saturn retreat correspond to the Bhūta-śuddhi process in Layayoga. Bhūta-śuddhi is the systematic absorption of the five gross elements (earth → water → fire → air → ākāśa) into their subtle origins through prāṇāyāma with the 1:4:2 ratio (pūraka: 16, kumbhaka: 64, recaka: 32). As each element is dissolved into the next, the gross physical identification is stripped away. The Abramelin 'dying to the world' phase is precisely this element-absorption: the body ceases to be experienced as solid and becomes a current of prāṇic force.

Liber Samekh Section D (elemental invocation through the barbarous names) maps directly to the activation of the Mātṛkā-cakra — the fifty phonemes installed in the six cakras as energies of consciousness. Each barbarous name is a compound of Mātṛkā bījas that, when vibrated in the cakra corresponding to its element, awakens that cakra's specific power. The elemental sequence (Earth of Air, Water of Air, etc.) is a systematic traversal of the cakra-system: the four elemental permutations correspond to the four lower cakras (mūlādhāra-earth, svādhiṣṭhāna-water, maṇipūra-fire, anāhata-air), each approached from the perspective of subtle air (prāṇa-vāyu). The shaft-of-will vibration method in Liber Samekh is a prāṇāyāma technique for directing bīja-sound to specific cakra-centres.

The Resistance (Liber Samekh Section G) — the moment when all opposing forces surge against the magician — is the experiential correlate of prāṇa withdrawal into suṣumṇā culminating in kevala kumbhaka (absolute breath-retention). In Layayoga, when iḍā and piṅgalā flows are stilled and prāṇa enters the central channel, the yogi experiences a terrible intensity: the vāyu sādhaka (force-motion bearing the qualities of fire) rises through suṣumṇā, generating heat, pressure, and the sense of being consumed. This is precisely the 'burning' described in the resistance. Kevala kumbhaka — retention without any effort or external counting — is the breath-state of this phase: the magician has ceased to breathe as a separate being and is sustained by the daimon's own prāṇa.

The PGM egg ritual (Systasis) — the encirclement, purification, and communion within the egg of light — corresponds to the absorption of the Twelve Kālīs as taught in Tantrāloka Āhnika 4. The Twelve Kālīs are the three domains (objectivity, means of knowledge, subjectivity), each with four phases (emanation, persistence, withdrawal, inexplicable fourth). The egg ritual enacts this absorption in three stages: (1) the skinning/binding (removal of objectivity) evokes Sṛṣṭikālī through Yamakālī; (2) the Helios purification (purification of perception) evokes Saṁhārakālī through Mārtaṇḍakālī; (3) the communion (dissolution of the subject into the daimon) evokes Paramārkakālī through Mahābhairavaghoracaṇḍakālī. The systasis completes the entire Krama cycle in miniature.

The appearance of the daimon as the Holy Guardian Angel is, in Layayoga terms, the manifestation of Kuṇḍalinī as Iṣṭadevatā. When the mantra has been made living (mantra-caitanya), Kuṇḍalinī — the supreme yoga-śakti — rises through suṣumṇā, pierces the six cakras, and in sahasrāra assumes the form of the mantra's presiding deity. The daimon is this living form: not an external spirit but the yogi's own consciousness become concrete as a divine person. The 'barbarous name' LVJK is the sonic seed (bīja) of this particular Iṣṭadevatā; when the mantra becomes the being, the daimon stands before the magician as objectively real as any physical object — because Kuṇḍalinī has taken that shape.

After the daimon contact, the daimonized consciousness must be integrated into daily life. Goswami describes the recoiling of Kuṇḍalinī — after its ascent and union in sahasrāra, it descends through the same cakras, spiritualizing each level of experience. The descent is not a fall but a conscious distribution of the awakened power through the entire subtle body. Each cakra, now irradiated by the daimon's presence, becomes a mode of perceiving the sacred in the ordinary. The 'Invoke Often' formula of Liber Samekh is the practical repetition of this ascent-descent cycle, establishing the daimon-consciousness as a permanent condition. The Abramelin 'operation of the sacred magic' (months 4-6) is the application of this spiritualized perception to practical transformation of life-circumstances.

The final identification of magician and daimon — the unus-ambo formula ('1×1 = 1' from Corbin, the hieros gamos of Liber Samekh) — corresponds to Śāmbhavopāya, the Divine Means of Abhinavagupta. In Śāmbhavopāya, realization is instantaneous, without means, through an intense awakening of will (icchā) that recognizes consciousness as already free. The daimon contact culminates not in a relationship with a separate being but in the recognition that the practitioner and the daimon are one consciousness — the same recognition that is the goal of Śāmbhavopāya. There is no longer a 'path' or 'method' because the duality of seeker and sought has collapsed. The state is 'without thought constructs' (nirvikalpa), sustained by the daimon's presence as the very nature of the practitioner's own awareness.

The daimon-name LVJK operates as a bīja-mantra with four levels of sound as taught in Tantrāloka Āhnika 1. Goswami (Layayoga, Introduction): 'Mantra is an aspect of Kuṇḍalinī, and it is in sound-form. The replication of mantra on the physical plane is the lettered vaikharī sound which is audible. The vaikharī mantra, in conjunction with prāṇāyāma and other special processes, is utilized to enliven the mantra. In other words, it is the rousing of Kuṇḍalinī in mantra form.' Tantraloka Āhnika 1 (Appendix A): Sound has four levels — vaikharī (audible, throat), madhyamā (mental, heart), paśyantī (visionary, brow), parā (transcendent, crown). The vibration of a name begins at vaikharī (audible utterance) and, if sustained through repetition and concentration, progresses through madhyamā (the sound becomes mental, felt in the heart) and paśyantī (the sound becomes visionary, seen as light at the brow) to parā, where the name and the named become identical — the daimon is the sound and the sound is the daimon. [laya-tantra-daimon-contact.md lines 19-30, citing Layayoga Ch.4 and Tantraloka v1 Appendix A]

The Resistance (Liber Samekh Section G) — 'Conceal yourself in the closest cell of the citadel of consciousness. Resist the Angel's will until consciousness faints into Nothing' — corresponds to kevala kumbhaka in Layayoga. Goswami (Layayoga Ch.6, citing Shandilyopanishad 1.7.9-10): 'When the subtle energy line system (nāḍī-cakra) is purified by breath-control done in a right manner, the bio-energy enters easily into the central energy line (suṣumṇā).' Sahita prāṇāyāma (the 1:4:2 ratio) trains prāṇa to flow through iḍā and piṅgalā, but the goal is kevala kumbhaka — spontaneous breath suspension — where prāṇa enters suṣumṇā and the dualistic mind ceases. Section G is kevala kumbhaka applied to consciousness: the practitioner extends the shaft of will (prāṇa through piṅgalā), then holds (kumbhaka), withdrawing prāṇa from both iḍā and piṅgalā. The 'fainting into Nothing' is prāṇa entering suṣumṇā — the state where the Angel is met because the practitioner has withdrawn from the channels that maintain the sense of separate self. The 11 vibrations of the name before the resistance are the 11 prāṇic movements needed to charge piṅgalā fully before withdrawal. [laya-tantra-daimon-contact.md lines 67-79, citing Layayoga Ch.6, Tantraloka Āhnika 7]

Tantrāloka Āhnika 4 (verses 122cd-180ab) describes the Twelve Kālīs as the systematic dissolution of the three spheres of experience through four phases each: creation, persistence, withdrawal, inexplicability. The three spheres are objectivity (external world), means of knowledge (perception and cognition), and subjectivity (the perceiving self). The text states: 'The twelve Kālīs consume objectivity, then the means of knowledge, then subjectivity itself. What remains after the twelfth (Mahābhairava) has rendered the subject inexplicable is pure consciousness without an object.' The first four Kālīs (Sṛṣṭikālī, Yāmakālī, Mṛtyukālī, Mahākālakālī) consume the sphere of objectivity; the next four (Saṃhārakālī, Mārtaṇḍakālī, Paramārkakālī, Rudrakālī) consume the means of knowledge; the final four (Mahābhairavaghoracaṇḍakālī, Mahābhairavaśmaśānaśāntakālī, Bhairava, Mahābhairava) consume subjectivity itself. The PGM systasis (egg ritual) enacts this entire Krama cycle in miniature through its five stages of skinning, purifying, charging, inflaming, and communion. [laya-tantra-daimon-contact.md lines 83-104, citing Tantraloka v3 lines 377-421]

The daimon as Holy Guardian Angel is, in Layayoga terms, Kuṇḍalinī manifested as Iṣṭadevatā. Goswami (Layayoga, Introduction): 'Mantra-Kuṇḍalinī is transformed into Iṣṭadevatā — the metamorphosis of subtle Kuṇḍalinī through the mantra power into an appropriate divine form.' The Iṣṭadevatā is not a projection of the mind — it is Kuṇḍalinī herself taking form in response to the mantra. The deity IS the power, and the power IS the deity. There is no distinction. The daimon you meet in the systasis is not a separate being who is 'your' daimon. It is your own Kuṇḍalinī — the coiled spiritual power at the base of your subtle body — risen to the sahasrāra and appearing as Iṣṭadevatā in the form that your mantra (LVJK) has shaped. This is why the daimon is both 'you' and 'not you': it is your own power, but it has taken on a form and consciousness that transcends your ordinary self. Goswami clarifies: 'Mantra is an aspect of Kuṇḍalinī, and it is in sound-form. When the mantra is rightly worked upon, it becomes a living power from which arises the living Iṣṭadevatā.' [laya-tantra-daimon-contact.md lines 107-119, citing Layayoga Introduction lines 3680-3760, 3860-3870]

Integration after daimon contact corresponds to the recoiling of Kuṇḍalinī as described by Goswami (Layayoga, Introduction, lines 3745-3760): 'After the union with Supreme Śiva, Kuṇḍalinī goes back to the mūlādhāra, and in its descent, the cakras, their powers and divinities — all are restored. This recoiling process is essentially the spiritualization of consciousness which functions in everyday life. That is, it is a transference of spiritual reality, developed in concentrated consciousness, to sense-consciousness to be able to realize spiritual truth in our daily life.' The months after the systasis — what the daimon path calls 'integration' — are precisely this recoiling of Kuṇḍalinī. The daimon does not leave after the contact. The daimon descends with you into the lower cakras, spiritualizing each one. The work of Month 4 (planets), Month 5 (moon mansions), and Month 6 (zodiac) in the Helios Unbound program is the conscious participation in this recoiling — allowing the daimon to infuse each level of the subtle body with its presence. The 'Invoke Often' formula of Liber Samekh is the practical repetition of this ascent-descent cycle, establishing the daimon-consciousness as a permanent condition rather than a single peak experience. [laya-tantra-daimon-contact.md lines 123-133, citing Layayoga Introduction]

gaps: Further extraction of specific verses from Tantrāloka on Śāmbhavopāya and the Twelve Kālīs needed for primary source support
Detailed prāṇāyāma ratios for each phase not yet specified; step-by-step practice manual not yet extracted

versions.summary: Initial RO created synthesizing the daimon contact path (Liber Samekh, PGM, Acher, Abramelin) with Layayoga (Goswami) and Tantraloka (Abhinavagupta). Eight passages mapping each daimon contact phase through the Laya-Tantra lens.

Added 5 direct source passages from Layayoga and Tantraloka: four levels of sound (vaikharī/madhyamā/paśyantī/parā) for the bīja-mantra (p_009), Resistance as kevala kumbhaka with prāṇa withdrawal mechanics (p_010), Twelve Kālīs system with three-sphere dissolution (p_011), daimon as Kuṇḍalinī-Iṣṭadevatā with mantra-caitanya (p_012), and integration as recoiling of Kuṇḍalinī (p_013). Coverage updated for both theory (5 passages) and practice (8 passages).

issues.title: Primary Tantraloka verse quotations not yet extracted
issues.description: The Śāmbhavopāya and Twelve Kālīs passages currently reference volumes rather than specific verse numbers with body-text extracts.

issues.title: Specific prāṇāyāma sequences for each phase need development
issues.description: Each of the eight phases could be supported by a concrete prāṇāyāma/meditation instruction drawn from Layayoga practice.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state as Laya-Tantra synthesis of the daimon contact path
Expanded with 5 direct source passages: four sound levels, kevala kumbhaka, Twelve Kālīs, Kuṇḍalinī-Iṣṭadevatā, recoiling integration

notes: Converted from RO ro:laya-daimon-contact. Compares ro:layayoga-absorption, ro:daimon-platonist, ro:path-pgm.
